tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'Use a Bitsy Bug in 1/4, 3/8, or 1/2 ounce for extremely clear, extremely cold, or heavily pressured water. Great for downsizing to up the size of fish you catch.'
Hackney: 'Throw the Bitsy Bug when fishing an event directly following another tournament where fish have seen a lot of lures.'
Hackney: 'I use the Bitsy Bug a lot in river systems with smallmouth. They take the small jig better than a traditional jig.'

Hackney: 'Use a 3/4 to 1-ounce football head jig on 12-17 pound fluorocarbon to cover water and locate sweet spots along gravel bars or ridges with isolated stumps. Same slow presentation as a Carolina rig but only one knot.'
Hackney: 'Use the football head when deep fish are not eating the crankbait or a slow-rolled spinnerbait. It stays in contact with the bottom and has a larger profile to appeal to quality fish.'
Hackney: 'If I could have one jig, the Hack Attack would be it. I can make it look like a shad, a crawfish, or a bluegill - whatever they are feeding on.'
Hackney: 'Use the 3/8-ounce in extremely shallow or cold water when fish prefer a larger, slow-moving profile. Also good for swimming presentations and suspending fish.'
Hackney: 'The 1/2-ounce is my go-to - versatile enough for shallow targets, cover penetration, or 10-20 foot depths by downsizing line.'
Hackney: 'The 3/4-ounce is best for brushpiles in 15-25 feet.'
Hackney: 'Use 1-ounce or 1.25-ounce in really heavy stuff for a fast-falling presentation that bullies its way through cover.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'I carry more trailers than I do jigs because conditions sometimes change hourly. I can completely change the look of a jig by changing the trailer - from no action with a Chunk to looking like the trailer is propelling the jig with a Rage Craw.'

Hackney: 'I use a medium-heavy rod instead of a heavy action broomstick for flipping. With braid there is no stretch so you do not need a superstiff rod. The medium-heavy is more accurate, lets me feel the fish before it feels me, and a softer rod is better for fighting fish.'
Hackney: 'I use braid for 90 percent of my flipping. Its smaller diameter and limpness gives the jig more action. I prefer 50-pound red Cajun braid.'
